mcqs.question: What is the primary goal of a clickjacking attack?

options.text: To steal user credentials directly from a form
To execute malicious code on the server
To trick a user into performing an action on a hidden website
To perform a denial-of-service on a web application

mcqs.explanation: Clickjacking (UI redress attack) deceives the user into clicking on a hidden iframe, causing them to perform an unintended action on a legitimate site they are logged into, such as transferring funds or changing settings.

mcqs.question: Which HTTP header is considered the modern standard for preventing a page from being embedded in an iframe?

options.text: X-Content-Type-Options
X-Frame-Options
Strict-Transport-Security
Content-Security-Policy with the frame-ancestors directive

mcqs.explanation: While X-Frame-Options is widely used, the Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header's frame-ancestors directive is the W3C standard and offers more granular control, making it the modern recommended approach.

mcqs.question: What is the effect of the header 'X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN'?

options.text: The page can only be framed by pages on the exact same domain
The page cannot be framed by any website under any circumstance
The page can be framed by any website from the same country
The page can only be framed by a single, specified domain

mcqs.explanation: The SAMEORIGIN directive allows the page to be displayed in a frame only if the parent document is from the same protocol, host, and port (i.e., the same origin).

mcqs.question: Why is client-side JavaScript framebusting (e.g., `if (top != self) top.location = self.location;`) considered an unreliable defense against clickjacking?

options.text: It requires cookies to be enabled in the user's browser
It can be easily bypassed using the iframe sandbox attribute to disable scripts
It violates the same-origin policy and will be blocked
It only works in older versions of Internet Explorer

mcqs.explanation: An attacker can neutralize framebusting scripts by embedding the target page in an iframe with the `sandbox` attribute, specifically without the `allow-scripts` permission, thus preventing the JavaScript from executing.

mcqs.question: A web application sets both 'X-Frame-Options: DENY' and 'Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors https://trusted.com'. What will be the behavior in a modern browser that supports both headers?

options.text: The page will not load due to conflicting security policies
The page can be framed by https://trusted.com
The page cannot be framed by any site, including https://trusted.com
The browser will ignore the older X-Frame-Options header

mcqs.explanation: Modern browsers that support CSP will typically give precedence to the more specific `frame-ancestors` directive. However, since it conflicts with the more restrictive `DENY` (which maps to 'none'), the most restrictive policy is enforced. 'none' is more restrictive than a allowlist, so framing is completely blocked.
